CURRENT AFFAIRS.

- The first list of subscriptions to the Home Rule Fund
amounts to no less a sum than five thousand four
hundred pounds odd. ‘Seventy-two Nationalists Mem-

-bers of Parliament are responsible for £3,700 of this
total, as they subscribed £50 each, Gussy Roche sub-
scribing £150.  This acticn of the members is generous
~ and inspiring. They are paid £400 each now, but it
hard to part with money that has once found its way
our pocket. They receive the money as a matter

urse, and they are entitled to stick to it, and they

or the old fighters at least ave—entitled to say that

le enough, considering all the years they spent

vith y financial recompense beyond

what some of them received out of the voluntarily sub-
scribed Parliamentary Fund.  Indeed, the M.P.’s, by
their subscribing per cent: of their salaries to the
National Fund, rather place the various prominent
Gaelic Leaguers who have secured lucrative posi-
tions in the National University on their de-
fence; and it is now up to the latter to show
that in the matter of the Language Fund, they are
at least equal to the M.P.’s in the latter's relation to
the Home Rule Fund. There are others who are in
receipt of incomes from the National University whose
interests are more in the political than in the language
cause, and we would expect them to generously follow
the lead of the M.P.’s.  Professor Kettle need not be
reminded by us—for he is a brilliant man at figures—
that 12} per cent. on his income is £62 10s., and that
ought to be his subscription to the Home Rule Fund.
Professor Donovan might be expected to give at least
fifteen per cent. of his income; but nothing less than
50 per cent. would, we think, meet the case of Professor
Oldham. Professor Oldham stands by himself for two
reasons. In the first place, commenting on the extra-
vagance of official expenditure in Ireland, he said re-
cently in effect that he would be glad to take the job
he now holds at half the money ; and in the second place
he dramatically stated at Mr. Childers’ lecture—and
a great actor was lost in the dismal professor—that he
was out for Freedom and not for money. Under these
public circumstances we think that the dismal professor
could not reasonably contribute less than £300 to the
Home Rule Fund.

The militant Suffragettes appear to have cooled off,
and we hear very little of the imitation Suffragettes in
this country just now. The performances of some of
the latter at the Churchill meeting have rather got the
country’s back up against them. Kettle, Cruso and
Skeffy do not appear anxious to earn jail in the cause,
and in that they are unlike the real English male Suf-
fragette, Mr. Laurence. There is one consideration
that tempers our disgust with the imitalion Suffragettes
of this country, and that is our utter disgust at the
degeneracy of some of the so-called young men who are
knocking about. Ladies’ fashions will be left in the
lurch shortly by young men's fashions.  The limit is
the watch bracelet for chappies. We sat near one of
these Johnnies on top of a tram on Sunday, and when
we saw the watch bracelet round his wrist we felt like
sympathising with the militant Suffragettes. If one
of them threw a hammer at him we would not have
interfered. Canary gloves appear to be another fashion
for Johnnies who certainly deserve to be smacked by
the Suffragettes. Canary gloves and watch bracelets
are a disgrace to the male sex. ~We think we discern
a fashion in walks also. We heard recently of a young
man’ who imitated the walk of Tim Healy! but that
is not the sort of silliness we refer to now. There is a
sort of damn-it-all lounge prevailing. It appears to
suit some canary-gloved Johnnies, but we recently had
a pleasant sixty seconds observing a youthful B.L.
laboriously trying to imitate the lounge. It is no
wonder there are Suffragettes when creatures with the
outward semblance of men wear watch bracelets and
canary gloves. We fear that a man with canary gloves
would be shot at sight in Texas; and we suppose &
creature with a watch bracelet would be lynched right
away. After all a combination of whiskers and
knickerboclkers is a few degrees higher than a combina-
tion of watch bracelets and canary gloves. 2

The “ Eveninﬁ Telegraph '’ came out very strongly
some years ago when Pensioner (then Mr.) Yeats was pro-
secuting people in the Dublin Police Court for express-
ing disapproval of the outrageous ¢ Playboy.”’ One of
those prosecuted—and we think heavily fined—was the
father of Mr. Colum, the minor poet, and another was
. Mr: Beasley, who at the time was, we believe—
he certainly is now—on the staff of the ‘* Freeman * or
one of its papers.  Since then Pensioner Yeats has
hawked the thing in America, under police protection,
and to the accompaniment of police arrests and prosecu-
tions, and a ‘‘ welcome " is being enginecered for the
Pegeen-Mikes on their reurn from their reprehensible
campaign in America. The ‘‘ Freeman '’ of Saturday
gives the ““ welcome’’ a puff. We read: “ As will be
seen by our advertising columns a reception, etc.”’
The ““ Freeman ”’ puff tells us that  an influential com-
mittee, as diverse in politics and opinions as could any-
where be found, has been active in organising the com-
ing function, ete.”” The poor ““Freeman'! We
turned to the names of the committee, as advertised,
who are trying to sell “‘ welcome > tickets at half-a-
crown each.  They are mostly composed of Dublin
Castle hunters, tames and norientities: we have already
seen that Mr. John Sweetman was asked to join, but
publicly replied that he would join in a protest against
the Pegeen-Mike business if one were started. The
first name on the list is that of Lady Arnott. There
are several other titled women, Lady Lyttleton, Lady
Nixon, Lady Shaw and Lady Thompson —all household
words, not only amongst the patriotic Irish at home,
but amongst those abroad! The first male name on the
list is that of the Rt. Hon. Bailey, a gay spark who
seems to be at everything in the way of a Dublin Castle
function or a suburban “ At Home ’’; we wonder how
he finds time to earn his big salary by doing any Land
Commission work. Who is Sir John Barton?” Who
is Mr. Charles Chaytor? Sir Hugh Lane is, we belicve,
a relative of one of the Pegeen-Mike syndicate, so the
presence of his name is understandable. Dr. W. Lom-
bard Murphy is a son of William Martin Murphy, * the
Nationalist,”” and he appears to be always on the trot
after Dublin Castle functions and ‘“ At Homes.’” There
is not the name of one member of the Irish Parliamen-
tary Party, not one member of the Executive of the
Gaelic League, not one name of a prominent member of
the Gaelic League, not one of a prominent Nationalist
politician, not one of a prominent name of a mere Irish-
man of any kind on this committee engaged in engineer-
inga “ welcome’’ on the return of the ‘ Pegeen Mikes’’
from their foul campaign in America. The names of
the committee, mostly of nobodies, and utterly unre-
presentative, indicate the difficulties that were met with
in the endeavour to beat up a ‘‘ weleome.”  And all
these names are so many baits to catch half-crowns—
for Tom, Dick and Harry can go to the * welcome ™ if
they care to buy a ticket. We know that Mr. John
Sweetman was asked to lend his name to this com-
mittee and that he refused; but we do not know how
many others were sought and found not willing.

We tale it that the view of Pensioner Yeats is that our-

.green people are a weak people, and that he can force
- his will on them. If that be his view we admit he has
- some reason for holding it. We have seen the ‘‘ Free-
man’} puffing this reception, whilst a few years ago it,
or at least its “‘ Evening Telegraph,’’ was gaining even
popularity by its expressions of indignation at the
Pegeen-Mikery business. The Pensioner has apparently

The Royal College of Physicians of Ireland is situate
in Kildare Street, Dublin, and the chairman of that
College has a mallet. The mallet is white, and on the
face of that white mallet—if a mallet may be said to
have a face—are black marks; and these black marks
stand for nothing other than the square and compass of
that pernicious secret society of the Freemasons.

We are not very well informed concerning the college
affairs of Sawbones in Ireland. There are in Ireland
many patriotic doctors, but the medical doctors in Ire-
land in the lump are bad, we fear, from the Trish Tre-
land point of view. Fees and shoneenery are first, we'
fear, and patriotism is very much an also ran. We do

not know what is the significance and constitution of

the Royal College of Physicians of Ireland, but we see
by the Directory that its Fellows number sixty-nine.,
And the hammer of the chairman of this College is illu-
minated, if we may use that word, by the Freemason
arms, or whatever they call them. Sir Christy Nixon
is one of the Fellows, and he is Vice-Chancellor of
National University.  Sir J. M. Redmond, M.D., is
another.  Among the other Fellows are Dr. Andrew
J. Horne, Dr. Joseph O’Carroll, Dr. Cox, P.C.; Dr.
Martin Dempsey, Dr. E. J. McWeeney, Dr. W. J.
Dargan. And the hammer of these men’s chairman ig
decorated with the square and compass. Have none

of them the pluck to protest, or are they satisfied tobe |

ruled by a Ireemason hammer?

THE NIGHT WIND.

Angry blasts in the woodlands waking
Rave and roar,

Bold as buffeting billows breaking
On the shore,

Tossing trees in wan moonlight showing

Swaying swung by the wild winds blowing
Never still.

Pause nor peace not an instant knowing
Save his will.

Sad, ah sad, as the surges’ sounding
Is their cry.

Seem they soul$ of the dead years hounding
Memory |

Sighs long sown in Time’s sealed abysses,

Tears long shed, and forgotten kisses,
Faces white,

Pangs half-seared, and fate-poisoned blisses
Of the night!

MILLINERY.

s

- Glows the fire, and the shadows darken
In the room,

All alone here I sit and hearken

. .. To that boom;

Sinking now, now afar off swelling,

Drear yet grand in its mighting knelling,
Comes the blast,

Tales of days long departed telling—
Oh the past!

All night long I can hear it moaning
As in pain.
B ! Furious now, now in torture groaning
Once again. -
Far away o’er the forest bowling
Towards us, now gathered forces rolling
Thund’rous sweep—
Thousand trees in chant-chorus tolling
Till I sleep!
Joun J. HayDEN.

On St. Patrick’s Night the employees of Messrs.
Caxroll’s tobacco factory, Dundalk, presented the pro-

, prietor of the firm, Mr. Vincent S. Carroll; with an

illuminated address. A concert and dance in connec-
tion with the firm was arvanged for that night, and
the occasion was taken to make the presentation to
the head of this, one of our too few big Irish manu-
facturing tobacco firms. In the course of the address
we read :—

We have always found you an ideal employer,
kind, genial, generous, thoughtful, and consi-
derate of the welfare of your many dependents,
quick to recognise merit, but slow to punish
faults, a ready friend to all in need, and one who,
by his innumerable kind deeds, has endeared him-
self to all of us, and cemented strongly the bonds
of friendship that should exist between employer
and employe. Recognising that the stability of
an industry depends on the cordial co-operation
of master and worker, we pray that the mutual
good feeling now existing may always endure, and
bearing in mind the many favours we have been
the recipients of at your hands, we hereby tender
you our most grateful thanks for past benefits,
and pledge you our unswerving loyalty for the
future.

Addresses, like unanimous resolutions, are overdone
in Ireland, but both can be important and significant
at times. It is indeed pleasant to read of such an
address from the Irish employees of an Irish manu-
facturer. It is some years ago now since we had the
pleasure of inspecting Messrs. Carroll’s factory in
Dundalk, and since then a new factory has been added.
During recent years Irish tobacco manufacturing en-
terprises have had to stand up against the situation
created by the Imperial Combine, and the success of
Mr. Carroll’s firm is a success that may give hope to
all interested in Irish industrial development.

Are there any motor-trams? When you see greab
loads of Guinness’s barrels of drink going along the
uneven roads on clumsy wheels driven by petrol, it
seems strange to an outsider that trams on tracks are
not driven by petrol and so do away with the neces-
sity of overhead wires.

There was an account in a daily paper last Friday

the afternoon.”” = We are not surprised on reading
some of the names of those present at this Tommy.
Atkins’ band function. Mr. Yeats, now that he is a.
British Government pensioner, is not out of place at
a Tommy Atkins’ band function, and we are not aware
that Lady Gregory ever professed herself an *‘ex-
tremist.”’  But what of Countess Markieviez? Surely
this leader of the ‘‘ Fianna ' is somewhat out of place
at a Tommy Atkins’ band function. -

why not ? .Fahy’s case was in the hands of the Super-

Whelan’s letter that there are not many cases to be -

dealt with latterly. Surely the Supervisor had plenty
of time to report to Sub-Committee on Fahy's claim
before the last Saturday of January. Anyhow it ap-
pears that no meeting was held. Whose fault was it ?
Supervisor’s or Sub-Committeemen ? It is for Lhen}-
selves to say. Poor Fahy travelled on that day SiX
miles from his home to find that no meeting was being
held. He got such a drenching of rain that he was
very near going to his reward in the land where old
age pensions are not needed. It is difficult to bring
home responsibility to an Old Age Pension Sub-Com-
mittee. If the regular monthly meeting is not held
it can always be said that no claim was reported by
the Supervisor. No one outside the sacred circle can
know. In a case like Fahy’s, which should have been
decided abt the January meeting, let the Supervisor
and Sub-Committeemen apportion the blame.  The
applicant lost the increase of some weeks’ pension, to
which he was in strict justice entitled. Who is going
to recoup his loss—Supervisor or Sub-Committeemen ?
Mr. Whelan wants to score a point on the question of
ignorance of the method of procedure, and he seeks to
immortalise the names of his Committee in the pages
of the LEADER as ‘ model ’ men. On which I venture
the following remarks. There are more serious draw-
backs than ignorance of the method of procedure,
namely, want of grit and interest in and sympathy
with persons and things Irish. This case under dis-
cussion will illustrate the matter. Faly’s circum-
stances have not undergone one iota of change since
his first application a few years ago for a pension. He
then got only 3s. a week. If this ‘ model * Sub-Com-
mittee showed themselves possessed of the above qua-
lities they would have fought for their poor neigh-
bour’s claim with more success against the canny
Scotch Supervisor who then ruled the roost in Car-
rick, and there would have been no need to make the
new application, which has met the approval of
the present respected Supervisor and the ‘model’
Sub-Committee, and secured for Fahy the full pen-
sion.”’

Amongst the large display of Irish goods shown last
week the enterprising and well-known firm of C. Bull,
Ltd., Church Furnishers, Dublin, had a unique
display of elaborately carved oak credence tables,
fonts, missal stands, prie dieus, etc., executed on
their own premises; also beautiful Irish poplin preach-
ing stoles in elegant Celtic design, hand embroidered
on the premises. Added to this was a large collection
of brass work, including candelabras, fonts, and mis-
sal stands, ete., also Irish manufacture. It is pleasing
to note that so many of the articles required for our
churches can be executed and supplied by the above
well-known firm.

The danger of the minimum wage sentiment in Eng-
land is that it may be linked with very bad judgement,
and so attempts may be made that will do a great deal
of harm and probably no good. If we were Englishmen we
would be very pessimistic. There are too many dirty
papers and too little religion of any kind in England to
warrant a hopeful outlook for the future of that un-
happy country. But after all England is not our
problem, and we are not interested in it.

The sooner we get Home Rule the better for the em-
ployers and capitalists of this country.  The British
coal strike adds another reason. English social and
economic sores are festering, and what the future has in
store for that country we do not know, but the sooner
we are isolated to the extent of having Home Rule the
better. The occupying proprietors of Ireland are the
first line of defence against any undue encroachment
on individualism; the agricultural labourers are indivi-
dual owners of property in so far as they have houses
and plots of land, and their demands on the labour
market need not be feared as revolutionary. In url?nu
industries there is to some extent a danger, as owing
to the absence of Home Rule and the absence of Ire-
land an entity as a practical reality, urban workers are
an easy prey to the class of thing which England
has developed. & .

We want labour reform in Ireland and reform in

_many things that concern the labouring population, but
we want it to be in consonance with Irish ideals and
Trish conditions, and the longer we are tied to England
to the extent that we are tied at present, the more we
will be open to inoculation by English ideas in
matters dealing with labour and other problems. .Under
Homeé Rule, there would be a chance of developing our
own coal-fields, our peat, and above all our water power,

The Bishop of Cloyne is naturally receiving many
congratulations on his victory over his cruel libeller in
the *“ Dundee Courier.”” At a meeting of the Joint
Technical Instruction Committee for the County of Cork
a resolution congratulating His Lordship was passed
unanimously.  The resolution was proposed by a non-
Catholic clergyman, Rev. Canon Courtenay Moore,
M.A.  Canon Moore, in the course of his remarks,
said :—

‘“ The verdict which Most Rev. Dr. Browne ob-
tained was bound to do good. He (Canon Cour-
tenay Moore) spent the early portion of his life in
the province of Ulster, and he lived: all his clerical
life for over forty years in Cork, and his own im-
pression was, when he heard charges of this kind
flung broadcast about the people of Ireland, that
the people who made those charges could not know
how happily Protestants and Catholics got on in
the province of Munster, and how much real
genuine good feeling existed between them.”

1t sounds very nice of the Protestant Canon to say
that, but how is it that the bigots in Ulster are so
ignorant of the tolerance of Munster? Surely the re-
cipients of that great tolerance—the ‘‘ scattered Pro-
testants ** of the'South and West—are largely to blame.
Where they do not join in the brigade of liars who are
maligning the Catholics of Ireland, they do little or
nothing to denounce the lying and the slandering that
is going on wholesale. ~Canon Moore also said:—

** That he could say for himself that during his resi-
dence of over forty years in-the Co. Cork he never
received anything but kindness and courteous con-
sideration.  During that long period the country
had been agitated many times. He remembered
the Rising of 1867, and he remembered the agita-
tion on Mr. Gladstone’s Church Act of 1869-70; he
remembered many agitations, and through them all
he never received anything but kindness and con-
sideration (applause).”

We read Professor Kettle’s article on *“ The Future of
Private Property.”” It covers twelve solid pages, and,
as we derived little or nothing from it, we may at least
compensate ourselves for our trouble by writing a para-
graph on it.  There is nothing original nor direct in
the article, it is an awkward parade of ill-digested read-
ing, sprinkled here and there with small jokes and big
names; the painful and palpable effort to ‘‘ show off ™
puts us in mind of a young clerk on a tram-car display-
ing his brilliant socks for the admiration of a few re-
sponsible but unostentatious real business men on the
opposite seat The brilliant joke that ‘‘ we must
not throw out the baby with the bath’’ has probably
been made' before, for .it sounds like an old English
music-hall chestnut. - There is at least one humorous
thing in the article, though the humour is utterly
unconscious, and the smile is at the expense of the
essayist. Mr. Kettle says:—*‘ Private ownership may
be, if you will, the carrot dangled before the donkey to
keep him on the trot. But it is not wise to forget that,
in the diurnal grind of life, most of us display a marked
resemblance to that patient animal.”” The italics are
ours.

acts, which we are told was excellently played by the
students.

Why not establish a good corps of the Hibernian
Boys’ Brigade in Queenstown? A new phase of Angli-
‘cisation has started in Queenstown in the shape of Boy
Scouts, or scuts, rigged out in all the furqlliur Baden
Powell paraphernalia. They were marching through
the town last Saturday. The Hibernian Boys’ Brigade
appear to be well started in Dublin, and the acquire-
ment of the Irish language looms prominently in the
programme. We see no reason why a Hibernian Boys’
Brigade should not at once be established in Queens-
town, in Cork and other places. We want to catch the
youngster for Treland, and particularly for Irish. Gaelic
League branches are rather for grown ups, and the
youngsters are not, we fear, trooping into the Irish Ire-
jand ranks in as large a volume as we would wish. The
Hibernian Boys’ Brigade appears to have great possibili-
ties. Now that Queenstown has its Boy Scouts—and
we can guess the kind they are—the formation of an
Trish Boys’ Brigade should be seen to without delay.

Last weelt we commented on the silly and absurd
testimonial that some people are inflicting on Boyle,
County Roscommon, in favour of a boy of the King-
Harman’s, who is coming of age next month. We
have heard since that the Major Murphy whc presided
at the meeting is *“ receiver *’ on the King-Harman pro-
perty. One of the committee is Dermot MacDermot.
The King-Harmans’ estate was once the property of
the MacDermots, and now a MacDermot helps to get
up a testimonial to a boy of the King-Harmans! This
Mr. Dermot MacDermot is a lineal descendant of the
old outlawed MacDermots, whose broad acres are now
in the legal possession of the King-Harmans. A mem-
ber of the MacDermot family writes us:—‘I am a_
member of the family, and would not, even if a pauper, -
disgrace my name and creed in so humiliating and un-
necessary a business.  Shade of the old MacDermot,
of the Curlew Mountain in 1599, behold your proud
descendant now."”’

One of the coolest pieces of impertinence that we
have seen for a long time is contained in three lines of
the English ““ Times,’’ of March 25th, which a London
reader has sent to us. The three lines contain the
announcement of a birth in a town in Connacht. The
The fact that a birth in Connacht is announced in the
columns of the English ““ Times *’ is not, perhaps, a
thing to be wondered at, there are so many importa-
tions and Anglicised persons in the province. But the
announcement winds up with ‘‘ Irish papers, please
copy.’’ Was there ever such cool impertinence? Some
person in Connacht pays for the announcement of a
birth in the English *“ Times *’ and ignores even poor
““ Alf Fox ”” and Ardilaun’s ‘‘ Mail,”’” and then requests
““ Trish papers, please copy '"—for nothing, of course!
We have a very poor opinion of our daily papers, but
they are not so far gone as to wag their tails at the
condescending request of this particular happy father.

A Belfast correspondent writes :— In reference to
the query on page 81 of your issue of 9th instant of some
person writing under the nom-de-plume of ¢ Puzzled,’
I regret that the columns ol your paper should have
Dbeen unwittingly made the vehicle of an attack upon
a citizen of this city universally respected, and upon one
who for the past forty years has been a champion of
Teaith and Fatherland, without the: covering shield of a
nom-de-plume.  Mr. Burke, at the meeting of the
Standing Finance Committee of the Queen’s University,
Bellast, moved a resolution that the request ol the Gaelic
League to create a Chair of Irish instead of a Lectureship
he granted, and their offer to contribute £100 a year
for three years be accepted. There was no person to
second his proposition, although there certain
pro-Irish (2) other members of t 1t. The
whitewashil olution was proposed and ed, with
Mr. Burke dissenting. The latter ntleman dissented
because he knew the slution was insincere, and that
the recommendation it contained would probably receive
attention at * Tibb’s eve’ from the Senate as at present
constituted. This view ol his has been amply confirmed

1C°
since by there heing no difliculty found or alleged to be
in existence when funds were required for proposed large
increases to the salaries of Demonstrators and others.
It is quite apparent to me, or to any person living in the
city of Belfast, that ¢ Puzzled’ can have no prominent
connection with the Catholics ol Belfast, or ° Puzzled ’
would have had no difficulty in obtaining the information
he required from the only body in Belfast which has the
right to claim to represent the Catholics, namely, the

Lxecutive of the United Irish League. There have been

many matters recently in connection with the Queen’s
University, and the Chair of Irish is not the least of
them, which required attention being drawn to in the
Press on behall of the Catholic body, and that body has
not yet seen any communication regarding same from
‘ Puzzled.” It would be advisable in future if ¢ Puzzled ’
would make his enquiries upon the spot and ascertain
the facts belore making allegations similar to that in
vour issue. Iven then, if only in common justice to the
parties concerned, I trust ‘ Puzzled > will pen his allega-
tions over his own name, so that the Catholics of Belfast
may ascertain the name and standing of their self-claimed
champion. A marked copy of the LEADER for Mr.
Burke is unnecessary at any time as he is a constant
reader of it.”

IRISH FARMERS’ BUTY TO IRELAND.

_B/E ANY \\o‘qldy wish for a more extended report of

Mr. Gill's speech to the farmers of Tipperary,
at Thurles, than the daily papers supplied.  His pre-
vious addresses to the farmers and labourers of that
county set most inspiring and practical ideals before the
whole country population, and his discourse last weels
seems to have been another interesting chapter.  His
exhortation to the farmers that the great duty they
owed the country is to enrich themselves, may seem
very obvious and very agreeable, but it is a trubh that
needs insistence.  They have not been placed, as he
reminds them, in a position of irresponsible ownership,
but they are the trustees of the nation; and they owe it
to the nation whose long agitation has won them their
security and prosperity to make the very best of their
industry—to avail of ‘all the means of instruction of
experiments and co-operation that the country is pro-
viding them; and to study and work to make their use
of the land most productive and profitable for them-
selves and the whole comrunity. = Most farmers, and
especially the younger men, are following this purpose
fairly earnestly and intelligently, with self-interest and
public spirit.

There are hardly as many incompetents and wastrels
in farming life than in any other class of occupation,
but they are a greater social evil, as their own failure
to prosper involves the disemployment and. poverty of
the labourers and tradesmen whom the land ought to
malke a living for if properly worked. Such individuals
will, however, be soon wed out, and there should hence-
forth be no toleration for the worthless man who is
neglecting his farm and getting into debt. The man
who has character and industry will always get capital
or credit enough to recover any temporary misfortune;
but ““ get on or get out "’ should be the national policy
towards the worthless or unreliable land-holder who is
blocking the prosperity of the country.

Similar conditions prevail for church music as for
operatic musie. Operatic music also malkes demands
on its interpreters which are not sufficiently provided
for in the curriculum of the ordinary music schools.
Hence it is that at nearly all the larger music schools
special departments have been instituted where con-
ductors and singers get an opportunity of preparing
themselves for the special’ demands of dramatic music.
Such considerations led to the establishment of
special schools for church music in Germany, Belgium,
and France, and these schools have been an important
factor in the improvement of church music that has
taken place in those countries. In Italy, too, several
such schools were founded. But still a need was felt
of a higher school of church music at the centre of
Christianity, and hence when the Italian Society of St.
Cecilia awoke a few years ago to renewed life and
vigour, thanks to the energy and practical ability of
its present President, Fr. Angelo de Santi, S.J., they
set about establishing such a school, and on 1st Jan.,
1911, they managed to make a beginning. The school
found a temporary home in the College of the ‘‘ Figli di
Maria Ss. Immacolata,’”” where their Superior-General,
P. Antonio Piccardo, generously placed some rooms af
the disposal of the school. They were able to secure
the services of some excellent teachers, such as Mons.
Rella, Vice-Director of the Sistine Chapel, for practical
instructions in Plain Chant; P. Gaisser, O.5.B., of the
Greek College, Rome, for the Theory, Prof. Baralli,
of Lucca, for the Paleography of Plain Chant, etc.
The school was frequented in its first year by 81 pupils,
18 of whom attended the whole course, and 13 the lec-
tures on Plain Chant alone. Amongst the pupils, ten
were foreigners, one cach from England, France, Ger-
many, Austria, the United States, Mexico, and Syria,
and three from Spain.
When the school had been started, the next step
of the promoters was to get official recognition. This
they got on November 4 of last year, through the follow-
ing letter of His Holiness, addresed to Cardinal
Rampolla, the Patron of the Society of St. Cecilia:—
““ The expectations We had formed have, We under-
stand, been fully realised: in the gratifying success
achieved by the students who attended the school
which was founded last year under your auspices by
the Pious Society of St. Cecilia, and to which Our be-
loved son, Antonio Piccardo, Superior of the Congrega-
tion of Mary Immaculate, gencrously gave the shelter
of his house. This higher school is attended by laymen
and, what is of greater moment, by the clergy, both
secular and regular, when they have completed their
course of theology and are free from all other occupa-
tions. Their purpose is to perfect themselves in the
science and art of sacred music, a matter to which We
devoted. Our attention at the very beginning of Our
Pontificate. The difficulty of imparting and acquiring
a satisfactory knowledge of sacred music in the ordi-
nary schools is obvious: in the ordinary schools the

Another great success was achieved by the school

these two notes. Whatever be Tacitus’ authority for
his statement, would not the meaning attributed to his
text in both notes make him responsible for a still more
‘“ strange and unaccountable statement’'?  Are not
balloons and airships of all descriptions of modern in-
vention?  Surely then Tacitus would not have told us
this very enlightening fact that *“ the coasts and har-
bours ’’ or ‘‘ ports and harbours '’ of an island are befter
known than the island itself. Does not this very chap-
ter refute such a theory? Tacitus places Ireland mid-
way between Britaiu and Spain and convenient to the
sea of Gaul; and since it would thus be ‘so much
nearer the Continent and so much more in touch with
civilisation,”” does he not ‘‘imply that the ports and
harbours were better known than those of Britain " ?
Would not this conclusion be more logical, viz., that it
was precisely because its ““ ports and harbours were
better known through commerce and merchants ’ that
Tacitus gave it such a position geographically? In
this connection let me quote a sentence from a very
modern book: ‘ Wexford, Waterford, Youghal, and
Cork harbours are opposite Milford Haven, so that the
fertile parts of southern Ireland are easily accessible by
the South Wales route’’ (‘“ The British Empire, Past,
Present and Future,”” p. 16. Londen. An. 1909).

left on me up to this is, that the anthor was writing in
furtherance of a theory. I am willing, quite willing to
admit that, it was unconsciously and unwittingly. But
to anyone reading the book with attention and care, it
would, I think, seem as if a lesson was to be drawn
from these chapters—that the Gauls and Britons were
civilised and cultured long before the Irish, that their
absorption by the Roman Empire accentuated their
civilisation and culture, and that, whatever historical
fact would seem to imply, a superior civilisation
amongst the Irish should forthwith have cast upon it
the ‘‘ défiance methodique.”

In the chapter on St. Patrick there is no reference to
St. Patrick’s Confession, which contains practically all
that is known with certainty of the Apostle of Ireland.
Nor does the author cite Prof. Bury’s work; while we
have reference made to Gibbon’s *‘ Rise and Fall of the
Roman Empire ** for facts which we would expect to be
sought for rather in the Life of St. Martin.

(p- 93).  These sentences are, of course, only i -

When Lughaidh (508), after twenty-five years’ reign.
was killed by lightning, he was succeeded by Muircher-
tach. In the “ Annals of the Four Mastors the first
battle (Dedna, 518) of Muirchertach is given in- the
tenth year of his reign.  Eleven years pass before
another (Ath Sighe, 524) is recorded, and two years
before any others. 1In 526 six battles are recorded but
three or four of these, in Kildare, Wicklow and Carlow
are probably only engagements in the one campnignz
He died 527 after twenty-four years’ reign, twenty-one
of which have no record—in the Four ﬁ[astars at any
rate—of battles fought by him. His death—he was
drowned in a vat of wine while endeavouring to escape
being burned—may have been accidental and not due
to f,he murderous designs of the woman, Sin. Her
various and significant names seem to point to a mythi-
cal personage rather than to a real one. - Be that as it
may, ln_s reign—if the Four Masters’ account be an
exhaustive one—can scarcely be termed one of * turbu-
lence and blood.’’

His successor, Tuathal Maelgarbh, was murdered
g.fter eleven years’ reign, during which only one battle
is recorded of him (Luchair-mor, 524) in the first year
of his reign.

Diarmuid, who was the next Ardri,.was slain in battle
after twenty years’ reign. But on y three other battles
are recorded of him—Cuilne, 546, in the eighth year of
his reign; Cuildreimhne, 555, in the seventeenth ; and
Cul-Uinsenn, 556, in the eighteenth year of his reign.

Donal and Fergus ruled jointly, fought two battles in
the first year of their reign, and both died peacefully in
the third year.

They were succeeded by Eochaidh and Baedan, who
likewise reigned jointly; and, though both were killed
after two years’ reign, no battle is recorded of them.
Nor has Ainmire, their successor, any battle record,
though he, too, was killed after three years’ reign.

Baedain, who next ruled Ireland, was slain in battle
in the first year of his reign.

The next Ardri was slain in battle (Dunbolg, 594)
after twenty-seven years’ reign, and only two other
battles are recorded of him—Doete, 572, in the fifth,
aqd Druim Mic Earca, 579, in the-twelfth year of his
reign:

Aedh Slaine and Colman Rimidh shared the govern-
ment “of the Island during the next six years, and,
though they both died a violent death the same year,
they have only one battle (Sleamain, 597) in the third
year of their reign to their account. .

Aedh Uairidhnach, who was the succeeding Ardri,
does not appear to have fought any battle, and after
seven years died peacefully.

Surely this is not a *‘ record of turbulence and blood”’
as far as the Ardris are concerned. During ten reigns,
extending over a period of about 104 years, we have
about ninety years free from battles and strife. That,
ab any rate, is the record as given by the ‘‘ Annals of
the Four Masters ” for the Ardris of Ireland during that
century. And there is no reason to think the reign of
the Ardri was more peaceful than that of any of the
provincial kings or chiefs. ‘‘ Nor was the Ardri, when
chosen, ever free from trouble, and of him it might be
said with truth that uneasy was the head which wore &
crown "’ (D’Alton, p. 92).” These are but samples of
the wild statements made, for which no reference is
given whereby to control them.

- The eighth chapter-is no exception to what we have
seen 5o far. Let me repeat what I have already said.

All through this chapter one thought seems to come
uppermost in the mind and recur to it again and again,
if the quarrels of the Trish were so frequent or so bloody
a8 the writer would have us believe, how could the
Irish'have so often defeated the Northmen? How could
they have even offered them any resistance at all, for

THE LEADER. 163

_fell in _battle, and their successors in the two following
~ centuries were pursued with similar misfortune ’

must not the nation have been spent after the period
of turb!.'\}euce and blood - that preceded; and, seeing
that the *“ turbulence and blood ** still continued dur-
ing the invasion, where did the Trish fighting-men come
from ? . How could this small island contain the huge
populatlgu that would have been necessary to support
suc_h sl.;x'lfe and war. Methinks the history of this
period is yet unwritten. No wonder ‘“ the Irish of to-
day have thémselves suffered by the calumny of their
de_nd. They alone among the nations have been taunted
with ancestors sunk in primitive disorders, incapable
of development; in the land they wasted '’ (“ The Mak-
ing of Ireland,” ete., p. x.) ‘*Irish annals are still in
our own days quoted by historians as telling merely the
tale of a corrupted land—feuds and battles, murderings
and plunderings; with no town or church or monastery
founded, no law enacted, no controversy healed by any
judgment of the courts. If the same method had been
followed for England, what an appalling story we should
have had. . . . In Germany or Italy or France the
picture of anarchy would appear like hell let loose on
earth. In all other histories, however, than that of
Treland a certain convention has been observed. Men,
by some high instinet of faith, have agreed amid all
disorders to lay stress on every evidence of reasonm,
humanity, justice, and to leave out of the record the
tale of local barbarities, the violences of the rich, the
brutalities of the ignorant and the starving. No human
society could endure in fact if these made up in any
nation the sole history of the people. In our country
alone the common convention has been reversed, and
the comparison of its culture with that of other lands
has been falsified at the outset ”* (Id., p. xi. sq.). Yeb
here again is a work professing to be History which
reverses the common convention, which makes a period
of “ turbulence and blood *’ out of ‘‘ the most flourish-
ing period in the history of the ancient Irish Church
(D’Alton, p. 171), out of the period when ““ Ireland
deserved to be called—as she has been by a great Eng-
lish writer—the School of the West ** (ibi.).

Under the title of *“ The First Christian Kings’' we
are treated to a feast of blood ; * The Danish Invasion’
serves up another dish of the same blood shed by the
same people, the Irish; ‘ The Dalcassian Kings " con-
tribute still another supply, and ““ A Long Period of
Discord *’ surprises us in showing that there is still a
supply left whencesoever it has come. From the
bloody feast we are led to the *“ Decay of Religion and
Learning”’ and a half-apologetic essay against Cam-
brensis in ‘“ Cultivation and Condition of the Arts.”
But of the Revival under Brian Boru we hear nothing,
nor do we know aught of the social condition or family
life of those clans of ‘‘ turbulence and blood,”" for the
description of a few ornaments can scarcely be called
the social history of the period.

After a close perusual of this book I must say my
advice to anyone seeking a clear, true, and trustworthy
knowledge of ancient Ireland before and up to the Nor-
man Invasion is, do not read D’Alton.

In writing these few notes I have been actuated by
no spirit of animosity. I do not know Canon D’Alton,
nor have I ever seen him. But I should not like a
work which may have a large civeulation both amongst
our friends and foes go before the world, belieing our
character, race, and nation. The Press reviewers
generally admire the cover, scan the table of contents,
and immediately write the critique ; they seldom trouble
to study the book and give an honest opinion.  Your
reviewers are, I am glad to say, an exception. If any
one thinks I have spoken with undeserving severity of
any portion of this part of the work, let him first study.
that portion, and I think he will find I have written
mildly. T trust that the object for which I wrote these
lines—and to write which T had to *‘ steal a few hours
from the night’’ and my sleep—will be realised. I
hope that someone with more time and ability will re-
view the book in a thorough, critical and impartial
manner, and let us have his impressions and results in
the columns of your valuable paper.

Seagin Cipeannac.
